meta.title: E28 M5 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW M5 E28 engine — M88/3, S38B35. Real UK costs, classic M-car ownership data, and honest advice on the original M5. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW M5 E28 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned E28 M5 engine typically costs £8,000–£14,000 supply only, depending on condition and specification — the M88/3 and S38B35 are similarly priced. Fitting adds £1,040–£1,300. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW M5 E28 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both the M88/3 (Europe/UK) and S38B35 (North America) are robust engines when properly maintained — but they require meticulous care. The timing chain guides are the single most important maintenance item, and regular valve clearance adjustments are essential. A well-maintained M88/3 or S38 can exceed 150,000 miles.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW M5 E28?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain guide wear (can cause catastrophic failure), valve clearance drift requiring regular adjustment, oil leaks from the valve cover and camshaft seals, and — unrelated to the engine — the occasional rust on the rear arches, sills, and jacking points common to E28s.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW M5 E28 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Reliability depends entirely on maintenance history. The M88/S38 engines are robust and well-engineered, but they are now 35+ years old and require specialist care. A well-maintained E28 M5 can be a reliable classic; a neglected one will be very expensive. Regular servicing, timing chain guide replacement, and valve adjustments are non-negotiable.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW M5 E28 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine variant is inherently bad — but avoid any M88/3 or S38B35 without documented timing chain guide replacement history. A high-mileage engine with noisy tappets and no valve adjustment records is also a risk. The M88/3 is the more desirable engine; the S38B35 is still brilliant but less powerful.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my E28 M5 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: A rattling or whining noise from the front of the engine (timing chain guide failure), a tapping noise (valve clearances out of spec), oil leaks that persist, or loss of power and poor running are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW M5 E28 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a £40,000 E28 M5, a £10,000 M88/3 recon is 25% — well within our 70% BMW threshold. Against a well-kept £60,000+ example, the same recon is 13–17% — a maintenance decision. On a concours car, an engine rebuild is simply part of ownership. On a sub-£25,000 project car, budget carefully — but even then, the rising values of E28 M5s often justify the investment.

mainEntity.name: How long do BMW M5 E28 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M88/3 and S38B35 can exceed 150,000–200,000 miles with proper maintenance — but the timing chain guides must be replaced proactively at 80,000–100,000 miles. Valve clearances must be adjusted every 15,000–30,000 miles. Track use accelerates wear on both engines.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the E28 M5 and the E34 M5?

acceptedAnswer.text: The E28 M5 is the first-generation M5 (1984–1988), powered by the M88/3 or S38B35 3.5-litre straight-six producing 256–286 bhp. The E34 M5 (1988–1995) is the second-generation M5, offered with the S38B36 (3.6L, 315 bhp) and later the S38B38 (3.8L, 340 bhp) — a more powerful and refined evolution. The E28 is lighter, more raw, and more collectable; the E34 is more practical and more powerful.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW M5 E28 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible on the engine itself. The M88/3 is found in European, UK and South African cars; the S38B35 is found in North American cars. Tell us your variant (M5) and market (UK/Europe or North America) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: BMW M5 E28 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the first-generation M5 E28 — from the M88/3 straight-six to the US-market S38. Real UK replacement costs, honest classic ownership data, and the hand-built M-division engine that defined the ultimate sports saloon.

engineDatabase.h2: BMW M5 E28 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the first-generation M5 — from the European M88/3 to the North American S38B35.

overview.intro: The E28 M5 is the first-generation BMW M5, the original sports saloon that defined the genre. Hand-built by BMW Motorsport GmbH from late 1984 to 1988, it was based on the E28 535i chassis but powered by a modified version of the M88/3 engine from the BMW M1 supercar — making it the fastest production saloon in the world at the time. The M5 was offered in left-hand drive for Europe, right-hand drive for the UK and South Africa, and left-hand drive for North America with a detuned S38 engine.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW E28 (second-generation 5 Series) • Predecessor: None — first BMW M5 • Successor: BMW M5 E34 • Sibling body styles in this generation: 4-door saloon only
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: As a classic icon that ended production in 1988, the E28 M5 is now a highly collectable enthusiast vehicle — the M88/3-powered UK and European cars are the most sought-after, while the S38-powered US cars are rarer but less powerful.

items.quote: The E28 M5's engine range is defined by one legendary powerplant — the M88/3 — and its North American sibling. The M88/3 3.5-litre straight-six (Europe and UK, 1984–1988) is the engine that started it all — 286 bhp, 340 Nm of torque, and a 7,000 rpm redline derived directly from the M1 supercar. It features individual throttle bodies, four valves per cylinder, and a 10.5:1 compression ratio — a genuine motorsport engine in a four-door saloon. The S38B35 (North America, 1987–1988) is essentially the same engine but with a lower 9.8:1 compression ratio and a catalytic converter, producing 256 bhp. Both engines are robust when maintained, but the M88/3 is the one that commands the highest prices and the most respect. If you're buying an E28 M5, the M88/3 is the definitive choice; the S38 is still a brilliant engine but with less power and a different character.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Timing chain guides, valve clearances, oil leaks
rows.ownershipVerdict: Best of Breed — the original M5 engine, now highly collectable

rows.commonMajorFailure: Timing chain guides, valve clearances, oil leaks
rows.ownershipVerdict: Solid Choice — detuned but still brilliant

economicsRule.title: The E28 M5 rule
economicsRule.text: A £8,000–£14,000 M88/3 recon against a £40,000 E28 M5 sits at 20–35% of the car's value — within our 70% BMW threshold. Against a well-kept £60,000+ example, the same recon drops to 13–23% — a maintenance decision, not a financial one. On a concours example, the engine rebuild is simply part of ownership. On a sub-£25,000 project car, budget carefully.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E28 M5 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M88/3 — maintenance is expensive, but the engine is robust when properly maintained.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E28 M5 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M88/3 — parts are increasingly rare and specialist-only.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E28 M5 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: M88/3 — the definitive first-generation M5 engine, worth the premium.

problems.title: Timing Chain Guides & Tensioner Wear
problems.rootCause: The M88/S38 engines use timing chain guides made of plastic that can become brittle and fail over time. When the guides fail, the timing chain can jump or snap, causing catastrophic engine damage. This is a well-documented failure mode on high-mileage M88/S38 engines. Regular inspection and preventative replacement are essential.
problems.recommendation: Replace the timing chain guides and tensioner proactively at 80,000 miles on any E28 M5 — a £1,500–£3,500 preventative job is far cheaper than the £8,000–£14,000 engine rebuild that follows a failure. This is the single most important maintenance item on the E28 M5.

cta.label: Compare M88/S38 engine prices for your E28 M5 →

problems.title: Valve Clearance Adjustments
problems.rootCause: The M88/S38 engines have mechanical valve lifters that require regular adjustment. If the valve clearances are not checked and adjusted, the engine can develop a tapping noise, lose power, and in extreme cases, suffer valve or camshaft damage. This is a specialist job that requires shims and precision.
problems.recommendation: Have the valve clearances checked and adjusted every 15,000–30,000 miles by a specialist who knows the M88/S38 engine. A £400–£700 service is cheap insurance against a £2,000–£4,000 cylinder head rebuild.

cta.label: Compare M88/S38 valve adjustment services for your E28 M5 →

problems.title: Oil Leaks & Gasket Failures
problems.rootCause: The M88/S38 engines are prone to oil leaks from the valve cover gasket, oil filter housing, and camshaft seals. The oil pan gasket can also leak on higher-mileage examples. These leaks are not catastrophic but can lead to oil starvation if ignored — and they make a mess of the engine bay.
problems.recommendation: Address oil leaks promptly — a £300–£800 gasket replacement is far cheaper than the £4,000+ engine rebuild that follows oil starvation. Have the oil level checked regularly and investigate any oil spots on the driveway immediately.

cta.label: Compare M88/S38 gasket replacement services for your E28 M5 →
